content: @Alice Ryhl how do I handle copying to/from user space pages that are mapped into the kernel? Page::write_rawand Page::read_raw require race absence, both on the page being operated on, and on the buffer being interacted with, I think. But there is essentially no way to guarantee this for pages that are mapped from user space.
Can I call it a black box, cover my eyes and do it anyway?
Does "Valid for reads/writes" mean race free?
Is this in a syscall from the process the page is mapped into?
If so, you could pass the address it's mapped into in userspace to the kernel::uaccess API

Yes. It is done by one of the read/write paths into the kernel. Can be regular read/write syscall, but can also be aio, uring or possibly others. The mapping code is the same. Essentially, the pages of the user space buffers are mapped into kernel space and I see them later at the bottom of the block stack.
And you are given a struct page for the memory?

I don't have a UserPtr. Essentially the kernel IO layer travels the user process mm, figures out which page corresponds to the address of the buffer and then maps it to kernel. Later I get a struct page pointer.
Rust doesn't really have a way to copy data that could be concurrently modified. The uaccess API falls back to doing the copy in asm.
How does uaccess get around the no race limitation?
With assembly.
right
uaccess also has some logic to deal with page faults if the address isn't mapped
That is already guaranteed for me
What does C do?
I was thinking about calling directly into C memcpy

Alice Ryhl said:

What does C do?

C just copies the data, no questions asked. No semantics is assigned to the data, it is just a move.
Then I guess you just do that too. :woman_shrugging:
But I will fail the safety requirements of the page functions.
Which takes me back to the safety requirements of ptr::copy_nonoverlapping
They just say valid for read and valid for write for length.
Does that imply no races?
Because if not, I think we can relax the requirements on the page functions.
As long as no references are made to the data in the pages.
The stdlib function copy_nonoverlapping absolutely does require no races.
It dosen't say so in the safety requirements
You're going to use it anyway because linux kernel memory model yolo.
My guess is that it's implied by "valid for writes"
And it should not, because it does not interpret the data it mvoes
Like C memcpy, it should not care
What do you mean?
I mean, since it is not creating references to src or dst, it does not have to require race absence. It essentially compiles down to a call to C memcpy.

Rust does not have any way to read a value that may be concurrently written to. If you think you found a function whose docs say otherwise, you can file a documentation bug with the stdlib. It does not mean you found a way to do it.
Data races don't care about references. It's about when you perform reads or writes to bytes of memory.
hmm
But if I call into a C or asm function that does the move, I should be fine?
I mean, we got the linux kernel memory model yolo just-do-what-c-does, so you can just do the memcpy in Rust.
To be completely correct, Rust is of the opinion that calls into C/asm can do anything Rust can, but no more.
But then how would you prefer I write my safety comment? Surely I cannot put "yolo".

/// Copy data of this segment into `dst_page`.
    ///
    /// Note: Disregards `self.offset()`
    #[inline(always)]
    pub fn copy_to_page(&self, dst_page: &mut Page, offset: usize, length: usize) -> Result {
        // SAFETY: self.bio_vec is valid and thus bv_page must be a valid
        // pointer to a `struct page`. We do not own the page, but we prevent
        // drop by wrapping the `Page` in `ManuallyDrop`.
        let src_page = ManuallyDrop::new(unsafe { Page::from_raw(self.bio_vec.bv_page) });

        src_page.with_pointer_into_page(offset, length, |src| {
            // SAFETY:
            // - If `with_pointer_into_page` calls this closure, it has performed bounds
            //   checking and guarantees that `src` is valid for `length` bytes.
            // - TODO: User space might write src_page at any time?
            // - We have exclusive ownership of `dst_page` and thus this write wil not race.
            unsafe { dst_page.write_raw(src, offset, length) }?;
        })
    }
Like, I can't just put "yolo" in this TODO
But if I call bindings::memcpy it's all fine? Then why not relax the requirements on the page functions instead.
You can explain that this is one of the cases where we don't follow the Rust rules precisely and rely on the linux kernel memory model instead.
Same as with DMA.
It's better to use bindings::memcpy() IMO ;-)

Alice Ryhl said:

You can explain that this is one of the cases where we don't follow the Rust rules precisely and rely on the linux kernel memory model instead.

More precisely, we rely on copy_nonoverlapping() behaving like a bindings::memcpy() in linux kernel memory model.
In practice I see it compiles down to memcpy anyway. I know it’s not a guarantee, but it seems like something to build on.
I’ll have to check out the reasoning in dma

Andreas Hindborg said:

In practice I see it compiles down to memcpy anyway. I know it’s not a guarantee, but it seems like something to build on.

We need to some confirmation from t-opsem, or as least let them know "hey we are doing this"
Haven't we gotten that confirmation countless times?
but in this case, the racy accesses are from outside kernel, so it may already be not a UB.
They told us to like C when Rust doesn't provide a mechanism.

Boqun Feng said:

but in this case, the racy accesses are from outside kernel, so it may already be not a UB.

That should not matter?

Alice Ryhl said:

They told us to like C when Rust doesn't provide a mechanism.

C doesn't have a copy_nonoverlapping(), for volatile, yes, for copy_nonoverlapping(), I don't know.
User space process is basically just another thread to kernel

Andreas Hindborg said:

User space process is basically just another thread to kernel

But it's a different program, it's similar to read_volatile() is not a UB if hardware is changing the memory at the same time.
Especially when we map their address space into our address space

Boqun Feng said:

Andreas Hindborg said:

User space process is basically just another thread to kernel

But it's a different program, it's similar to read_volatile() is not a UB if hardware is changing the memory at the same time.

I agree it should not be UB, but there is nothing in rust saying that it is not.
that's why I said we need some confirmation

Boqun Feng said:

that's why I said we need some confirmation

Right. How to get that? What is the best mechanism for contacting them? Do you already have a point of contact?
We know that we can treat volatile accesses are the same between Rust and C, but we don't know whether copy_nonoverlapping() is volatile or not

Andreas Hindborg said:

Boqun Feng said:

that's why I said we need some confirmation

Right. How to get that? What is the best mechanism for contacting them? Do you already have a point of contact?

There's a t-opsem channel in Rust zulip
The copy_nonoverlapping() method is not volatile.
If we call out to c memcpy it will be volatile
Unless lto

Alice Ryhl said:

The copy_nonoverlapping() method is not volatile.

It means two copy_nonoverlapping() with the same inputs can end up with only one memory copy after optimization?
"unless lto" that's saying it's not volatile

Boqun Feng said:

Alice Ryhl said:

The copy_nonoverlapping() method is not volatile.

It means two copy_nonoverlapping() with the same inputs can end up with only one memory copy after optimization?

Yes of course.
But really rustic should not optimize pointer operations
Of course it should.
Sigh. It makes things difficult
That's why we should be careful about relying on copy_nonoverlapping() behaving like memcpy() in kernel C code.
Even without lto, llvm knows about memcpy and will optimize assuming it behaves as specified.
Well, the existing C code in the kernel is relying on llvm not removing memcpy calls
It's good to have the optimization sometimes, it's just for your case, you want to avoid that.
I mean, it can only make optimizations that don't change behavior, so it's pretty hard to break a single copy from one of these user pages.
But if you copied twice, I'm sure it could merge them.
No, I don’t think so, but I need to check
The point is the semantics here really requires volatile, i.e. you want to compiler to generate memcpy asm regardlessly.
In rust idk, but I would be very surprised if that happened in c
Can I blackbox it or something?
I seem to recall there is something like that in std
What you want is a volatile copy. You certainly don't want the black_box thing.
https://doc.rust-lang.org/stable/std/intrinsics/fn.volatile_copy_nonoverlapping_memory.html
But there's no stable counterpart.
So this is related to ensuring llvm does not remove the call if it thinks it can. Not about potential races, right?
Well, the potential races makes it UB.

OK. What I am hearing is that

  • it is probably alright to rely on the current page functions that use copy_nonoverlapping but it would be better to call bindings::memcpy directly, although both give the same result.
  • None of that is volatile, but as far as I can tell that is not a requirement for my current situation.
  • I might have to contact t-opsem to let them know we are doing this, but I also might not have to contact them because they gave us card blanche to “just do like C” during the discussion of atomics and memory models.
And DMA has the same problem so I should look for how to write my safety comments over there
@Alice Ryhl I mentioned you by name here as author of statements you made in this thread.
If these potential data races are a problem in in dma and block direct io, then they are a problem in the uaccess module as well, right?
Or do they just rely on bindings::copy_from_user magically making the problem go away?
I don't see the difference of that and caling memcpy, even thouhg copy_from_user ends up in hand coded assembly.
